chunk_summary: This section outlines an enteral feeding workflow for pediatric CKD and CLD, focusing on formula densification, intolerance troubleshooting, and escalation to tube feeding when intake fails to meet prescribed nutritional targets.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of monitoring caloric and protein intake in pediatric CKD and CLD patients.
Learn stepwise methods for formula densification to address fluid restrictions and feeding fatigue.
Identify criteria for transitioning from NG to gastrostomy feeding based on intake failure and disease progression.
key_facts: Intake <80% of prescribed kcal/day and protein/day for 10–14 days is a nutrition emergency.
Densify formulas incrementally: 0.67 to 0.8 kcal/mL, then 0.8 to 1.0 kcal/mL, and up to 1.2 kcal/mL for severe fluid restrictions.
Monitor stool and vomiting within 3–7 days after densification to prevent intolerance.
NG feeding is preferred for short-term support (weeks to months), while gastrostomy is recommended for long-term needs (>2–3 months).
Recurrent admissions or oral aversion are indications for early gastrostomy referral.
reference_quote: If intake is <80% of prescription for more than 10–14 days despite symptom control, treat this as a nutrition emergency: densify now and start tube-feeding planning with a defined reassessment date.
document_key_topics: Enteral feeding in CKD and CLD
Criteria for NG versus gastrostomy feeding
